titleStmt.title: No. 346Memorandum of Conversation, by the Officer in Charge of Arabian Peninsula-Iraq Affairs (Fritzlan)

div.head:
No. 346
Memorandum of Conversation, by the Officer in Charge of Arabian Peninsula-Iraq Affairs (Fritzlan)
div.p: Mr. Byroade expressed to the Aramco representatives our grave concern over developments in the Buraimi issue.For documentation on U.S. interest in the British-Saudi Arabian dispute concerning Buraimi, see Documents 1466 ff. He said we were feeling increasingly at a disadvantage because we did not have a full understanding or appreciation of British objectives in the Persian Gulf area. He added that he planned shortly to go into this matter with the British Embassy and take a firm line. He was prepared to say that while we had backed them up to a great extent on the Buraimi issue in the past, we felt we must now know more precisely what their policies and objectives were in the area in order that our own long-term policy could be formulated accordingly.

Mr. Duce expressed appreciation and repeated the statement made often by Aramco representatives that the present critical stage of the dispute had been arrived at only because of British insistence to continue operations in the disputed area while Aramco has continued to maintain a policy since the Stobart incident in 1949 of not entering the disputed area.

Mr. Byroade stated that he had had recent talks with Mr. Skouras, movie producer, who is generally interested in the welfare of Greeks in the United States, concerning the Onassis agreement. It has been clear from these talks that Mr. Onassis is rather worried about implications of the agreement and would like to reach an understanding with the oil companies. Mr. Byroade mentioned that he had obtained a copy of the agreementSee footnote 1, supra . and had recently decided to take advantage of an opportunity to talk to Mr. Onassis. This he and Mr. Hart had done the previous day, and during the discussion Mr. Onassis indicated readiness to attempt to renegotiate the agreement if a formula could be worked out which would appease the oil companies without laying him open to the charge of breaking faith with the Saudi Government. In this connection he intimated he would be prepared to urge the Saudis to agree to a provision limiting his total offtake to a certain percentage. In any event, Mr. Onassis was prepared to give liberal interpretation to the agreement and in this regard he stated that it was his understanding that Article 4 of the agreement would not preclude replacement of tankers presently in use. He stated further that the provision in the agreement regarding rates was more or less fictitious and should not be considered necessarily binding.

Mr. Duce was happy to receive this information and stated that at this moment Mr. Onassis, on his own initiative, was discussing his agreement with Aramco representatives.An Aramco official reported to the Department of State that Onassis had been friendly and conciliatory at his meeting with Aramco representatives. He also reported information from Aramco representatives in Saudi Arabia that the Onassis agreement had been amended. (Memorandum of conversation, June 2; 886A.2553/6–254)

Mr. Byroade suggested that Aramco consider the possibility of coming to some arrangement with Mr. Onassis, since it appeared that they were to some extent at his mercy. Mr. Byroade stated further that our legal people were doubtful that Aramco had a good case on the matter of the Onassis agreement conflicting with the terms of their concession agreement.
